Drill Name: Gate Drill Centre Strike  3 ft (L to R)
Description:
Find the Line: Locate a small breaking Left to Right 3-foot putt on the green to ensure zero break.
Anchor the Putter: Place your putter head behind the ball as if you are ready to stroke it.
Set the Gate: Insert two tees into the ground—one just outside the toe and one just outside the heel of the putter.
Adjust Clearance: Start with about 0.5cm of space on either side of the putter head.
The Test: Ensure the putter can pass through the "gate" without clicking the tees; as you improve, narrow the gap to increase the difficulty.

Drill Name: Gate Drill Centre Strike  3 ft Straight
Description:
Find the Line: Locate a dead-flat 3-foot putt on the green to ensure zero break.
Anchor the Putter: Place your putter head behind the ball as if you are ready to stroke it.
Set the Gate: Insert two tees into the ground—one just outside the toe and one just outside the heel of the putter.
Adjust Clearance: Start with about 0.5cm of space on either side of the putter head.
The Test: Ensure the putter can pass through the "gate" without clicking the tees; as you improve, narrow the gap to increase the difficulty.

Drill Name: Gate Drill Centre Strike  3 ft (R to L)
Description:
Find the Line: Locate a small breaking Right to Left 3-foot putt on the green to ensure zero break.
Anchor the Putter: Place your putter head behind the ball as if you are ready to stroke it.
Set the Gate: Insert two tees into the ground—one just outside the toe and one just outside the heel of the putter.
Adjust Clearance: Start with about 0.5cm of space on either side of the putter head.
The Test: Ensure the putter can pass through the "gate" without clicking the tees; as you improve, narrow the gap to increase the difficulty.

Drill Name: Gate Drill Start Line 3 ft (L to R)
Description: Find a 3ft small breaking Left to Right Putt. Set two tees 3 inches in front of the ball, just wider than the ball’s diameter. Goal: Roll the ball through the "start gate" without touching the tees. Focus on a square face at impact to ensure the ball starts on the intended line.

Drill Name: Gate Drill Start Line 3 ft Straight
Description: Find a 3ft straight putt. Set two tees 3 inches in front of the ball, just wider than the ball’s diameter. Goal: Roll the ball through the "start gate" without touching the tees. Focus on a square face at impact to ensure the ball starts on the intended line.

Drill Name: Gate Drill Start Line 3 ft (R to L)
Description: Find a 3ft  small breaking Right to Left Putt. Set two tees 3 inches in front of the ball, just wider than the ball’s diameter. Goal: Roll the ball through the "start gate" without touching the tees. Focus on a square face at impact to ensure the ball starts on the intended line.
Chipping with your right hand only to build awareness and feels around the green. Feeling the body working with the right hand together
Chipping with your left hand only to build awareness and feels around the green. Feeling the body working with the right hand together
Cross handed chips to reduce the right hands input in the chipping motion. Great to build skills.
Place your arms as close to the seams on the shirt to try to feel like a t-rex. Using your pivot only to hit the chip shot.
Place a towel under your arm pit on both arms. This will help build the motion as one and build flow into the chipping motion.
Goal/Reps: 5 inside of 6 feet in a row

Description: Place a tee or towel 5 metres. Practice landing it on the spot taking in notes of what the length and feel is to generate that distance.
Goal/Reps: 5 inside of 6 feet in a row

Description: Practicing your flop shot technique and checking in on setup and fundementals.
Practicing your Putting chip technique and checking in on setup and fundementals.
Chipping over a bunker to a pin on the other side. This is to build comfort chipping over something to gain confidence.
Practicing your Bump and Run technique and checking in on setup and fundementals. Designate a club for this so you can get predictable results.

Drill Name: Up and down with every Club
Description: A 10m chip at a medium difficulty. Start with your highest loft club with one ball chipping and putting out. To go to the next level once you get up and down. Work your way up until driver.
Goal/Reps: Up and down to the next level.

Description: Play 18 short par2 holes up until 30m. Score is up and down = a par. Chip in is Birdie. Keep your score and try and beat it after you finish 18 holes
Play 9 short par2 holes up until 30m. Score is up and down = a par. Chip in is Birdie. Keep your score and try and beat it after you finish 18 holes
Practicing the mechanics to hit ball first. This will help you hit all lies.

Drill Name: One bounce, two bounce, three bounce
Description: The game is to land the ball off the green where it bounces once then rolls on the green. Then 2 and 3 times. This will help you control distance and trajectory.
Hitting drives of varying tee heights. From off the ground to very high. You will obtain skills in hitting the centre.
Create a fairway between targets. See how many times in a row you can hit it. Remember the percentage for when you are playing so you can make decisions on club selection.
Practice your low ball tee shot for windy days or getting the ball on the ground earlier to hit the fariway.
Hitting fades and draws back to back.
Fairway finders at 80 percent speed. Practicing precision over distance.
Not caring where the ball goes. Hitting it as hard as you can.

Drill Name: Hitting all parts of the face
Description: Intentionally hitting different parts of the face. This gives you awareness of the club and how to hit the centre or calibrate when on the golf course.
Designating a side that is an acceptable miss. Training a one way miss on purpose.
Gripping down different lengths on the driver to see how it effects ball flight and strike.
Hitting all 9 ball flights with driver. Straight,draw,fade,high,low,medium combined into the 9.
Use your video on your phone in the right positions and check your swing to see if its where you and your coach want it.
Practicing the high ball flight for maxium carry and down wind shots.

Practicing the opening tee shot of your next round or major tournament. Visualising the shot you need and hit the club that is most likely the club you will hit. Keep hitting shots and only remember the good ones for building confidence.
Practicing different divot sizes from picking it clean to a large divot. Noticing ball flight differences. Also use case scenarios when you would need to change it. Low point control.
Hit the same iron multiple distances on the range in 5 metre intervals. Great for the wind and also on course play.
Practice your low shot for windy days and recovery. Hitting extreme to a normal low shot. Pay attention to the systems that you use to generate the low shot.
Hitting grip down iron shots where you try different amounts of gripping down on the club and see the effects of flight and distance. Its the easiest way to take
Take a video of your swing with different clubs and make sure you are within the required positions to perform.
Hitting 20 balls while checking your set up including grip, ball position, stance, posture, alignment.
Hitting all 9 ball flights with driver. Straight,draw,fade,high,low,medium combined into the 9.
Designating a side that is an acceptable miss. Training a one way miss on purpose.
Stepping with the right root before the club moves second step with the left foot as the club reaches the top position. This gets the pressure shift happening correctly.
Practicing hitting between finger dispersions of your choosing. 1 Very hard, 2 Hard, 3. Medium, 4, Easy. To see if you can shot shape and still hit precise shots.
Hitting the same club over and over to see how many consecutive shots in a row you can hit between a 2 finger dispersion.
Hitting the same club over and over to see how many consecutive shots in a row you can hit between a 3 finger dispersion.
Performaing 3 different swing lengths. Shaft parallel, left arm parallel and full backswing. Building blocks for solid contact and make sure you can get all three in a row.
Visualling recovery shots on the range and performing them so you can translate them to the course better.
How many times in a row can you repeat the ball flight in a row. Main thing to look for is curve first then to get harder make sure they are starting on the correct side of the target.
Practicing the smoothest tempo you can do. If you have the tour tempo app this would be perfect as well.
Practicing every position on the clock and checking distances as reference points on the golf course.

Drill Name: Ramping Up Speed Full Wedge Swing
Description: Full wedge swings going from as slow as possible to as fast as possible while maintaining the same length swing.
Hitting low flight wedges. This will help you chase them to a back pin or windy conditions.
Hitting medium flighted wedges. Practicing the ball postion and swing mechanics to perform the medium, flighted wedge.
Hitting high flighted wedges. Great for stopping the ball on a dime when spin isnt an option.
Practicing cut spin wedges to work them into flags and use slopes to get the ball closer to the hole and stop faster.
Practicing draw spin wedges to work them into flags and use slopes to get the ball closer to the hole and stop slower.
See how griping down effects wedge distances and precision in your practice.
Practice the same wedge shot with a variety of ball positions to see the effect on spin launch and ball speed and how it can be used for certain shots.
Hitting wedges to see how many times in a row between the 2 finger dispersion
Hitting wedges to see how many times in a row between the 1 finger dispersion
Starting at 30m metres trying to land the ball between the one finger dispersion. Hitting 10m increments and seeing how many balls it takes to finish. Then trying to beat it again.
Hitting right hand only wedge shots to sync the arms and the pivot togther.
Hitting left hand only wedge shots to sync the arms and the pivot togther.
Placing a towel up high underneath the arms in the arm pivot to sync the pivot and the arms together.

Hitting wedge shots with no divots to steep divots to practice the skill of low point. And to see if you can perform them on command. See the differences of ball speed and trajectory/spin with each different divot style.
Practicing the left arm to left arm technique to make sure you are not overswinging the wedges.
Dialling in the 30m distance. Land it just before the 30m marker and just behind over and over again to learn how to hit the correct distance.
Dialling in the 40m distance. Land it just before the 30m marker and just behind over and over again to learn how to hit the correct distance.
Dialling in the 50m distance. Land it just before the 30m marker and just behind over and over again to learn how to hit the correct distance.
Dialling in the 60m distance. Land it just before the 30m marker and just behind over and over again to learn how to hit the correct distance.

Dialling in the 100m distance. Land it just before the 30m marker and just behind over and over again to learn how to hit the correct distance.
Hitting bunker shots just over the lip to get comfortable with landing the ball just outside the bunker without the fear of leaving it in.
Taking different amounts of sand to practice low point control.
Drawing a line in the sand and hover the edge just above it and hit exactly on that point. This will get your entry point better.
Practicing downhill bunker shots to see what things in your technique need to adapt for that lie.
Practicing uphill bunker shots to see what things in your technique need to adapt for that lie.
Practicing plugged bunker shot techniques from varying plugged lies to see how the ball behaves and if your technique can get it out.
Practicing above the feet bunker shots to see what things in your technique need to adapt for that lie.
Play 9 holes in different lies to see how you would score. Up and down counts as a par. Try and beat the last score and set a new record.
Practicing bunker shots with zero spin so they can roll out to the pin. This is one of the safer bunker shots.
Medium spin bunker shot that has a little bit of grab then rolls out.
High spin bunker shot that can spin backswards. This is a risky shot and should be a last resort.
Practicing a 5m landing bunker shot. Feeling the speed of the club and interaction of the sand as well as swing length to consistent perform the shot.
Practicing a 10m landing bunker shot. Feeling the speed of the club and interaction of the sand as well as swing length to consistent perform the shot.
Practicing a 15m landing bunker shot. Feeling the speed of the club and interaction of the sand as well as swing length to consistent perform the shot.

Practicing a 30m landing bunker shot. Feeling the speed of the club and interaction of the sand as well as swing length to consistent perform the shot.
Working through your oncourse routine with irons and driver. Timing yourself to get the closet time as possible.
Working through your oncourse routine with wedges and chipping. Timing yourself to get the closet time as possible.
Seeing yourself hit the perfect driving shot on a hole behind the ball before you hit the shot. Practicing the feeling of the perfect shot before you hit it.
Walking straight into the ball from behind and as soon as your feet touch the ground go. This gets you to be reactive and not be thinking through the swing.
Practicing the opening hole with recovery shots if needed. Getting you ready to play on the course. Having real outcomes on the range.
Hitting shots on the range for different wind directions, rain, cold and heat. Adapting decisions for the environment.
Hitting iron shots for the 18 holes on the golf course if you were to hit a perfect shot of the tee.
Playing the entire golf course's tee shots on the range to know you can hit the shot.

Start from one large step away from the hole and drop the ball. 2 Putts to get it in the hole. After holing out you now take 2 large steps away from the hole. Continue the process until you miss. Try and beat your record of how many strokes.

Set up a chalk line from 5 feet on a straight putt. Make sure you place a towel under your feet when doing bulk practice. Hit putts along the chalk line rolling the ball perfectly on it. This is how to practice start line.

Drill Name: Start Line Gate Drill 5 Foot Straight
Description: Place 2 tees one foot in front of the ball on the perfect starting line just bigger than the size of a golf ball so that the ball has to roll through them. Bulk practice hitting putts through the gate. Dont steer your putts let the gate teach you how to hit it straight.

Drill Name: Start Line Gate Drill 5 Foot R to L
Description: Place 2 tees one foot in front of the ball on the perfect starting line just bigger than the size of a golf ball so that the ball has to roll through them. Bulk practice hitting putts through the gate. Dont steer your putts let the gate teach you how to hit it straight.

Drill Name: Start Line Gate Drill 5 Foot L to R
Description: Place 2 tees one foot in front of the ball on the perfect starting line just bigger than the size of a golf ball so that the ball has to roll through them. Bulk practice hitting putts through the gate. Dont steer your putts let the gate teach you how to hit it straight.

Place a marker at the starting position and a target ball 5 feet away. The objective is to roll a putt that stops within one foot past the target ball without actually hitting it. If you successfully land the ball in that zone, move to the next attempt and try to continue the streak. If the ball hits the target or stops outside the designated one-foot area, you must reset and start over from zero. The goal is to achieve the highest number of successful putts in a row.

Drill Name: Just Short Just Long Just Right
Description: Place a marker at the starting position and aim toward a target hole at a chosen distance. Your goal is to complete a specific sequence of four putts in a row to develop elite speed control. First, hit a putt that finishes exactly one club length past the hole. Second, hit a putt that finishes exactly one club length short of the hole. Third, hit a putt that finishes a half club length past the hole, followed by a final putt that finishes a half club length short. If any putt in the sequence fails to land within the designated distance or strikes the hole, you must reset the entire process and start over from the first putt. The objective is to complete the full four-putt rotation successfully without any errors in speed or distance.

Select a target at a specific distance and prepare to hit a series of putts toward it. Immediately after making contact with the ball, close your eyes and visualize where the ball will stop based purely on the feel of the strike. By removing your visual feedback, you force yourself to become more conscious of the physical sensations and tempo required to control distance. After each putt, guess the result before opening your eyes to see how closely your internal feel matches the actual outcome. The goal is to calibrate your senses so that your perceived speed and the actual distance traveled become perfectly synchronized.

Place two tees the width of a golf hole exactly one foot in front of the cup, centered on your intended line. Your goal is to roll the putt through this narrow gate and into the hole without the ball striking either tee. This drill forces you to commit to a specific start line while ensuring the ball has the correct pace to hold its line through the gate. If you hit a tee or miss the cup, the attempt is unsuccessful and you must reset. The objective is to sharpen your short-range accuracy and visual focus by successfully navigating the gate and holing the putt consistently.

Set up a variety of putts from 6 feet and in at different locations around a hole to practice your AimPoint reads. For each putt, use your feet to feel the slope, determine the correct AimPoint finger read, and select your specific aim spot. Once the read is established, focus entirely on executing your routine and rolling the ball over your chosen start line with consistent speed. This drill is designed to refine your green-reading process and build trust in your numbers by seeing the ball consistently find the center of the cup. The objective is to complete a set number of successful putts while strictly following your read-and-react sequence to solidify your confidence from short range.

Select a series of mid-range putts between 8 and 20 feet on a green with various slopes. For these longer distances, start by standing halfway between the ball and the hole to feel the primary slope with your feet, then move to a point two-thirds of the way to the hole to check for any late changes in the break. Identify the highest slope percentage you felt (on a scale of 1 to 5) and return to a position approximately two feet behind the ball. Extend your arm and hold up the number of fingers corresponding to that maximum slope value, aligning the outside edge of your hand with the center of the cup. The inside edge of your fingers now marks your precise aim point on the green. Focus on starting the ball exactly on this line with enough speed to finish 12 inches past the hole. The goal is to trust the multi-point read and eliminate mid-stroke compensations, building confidence in your ability to marry distance and direction on mid-range putts.

Identify a series of long-range putts between 21 and 40 feet on a green with significant elevation changes or multiple breaks. For these longer distances, divide the putt into three equal sections and walk the line to feel the slope with your feet at the one-third, middle, and two-thirds points. Determine the average slope percentage across the entire distance and return to a position approximately three feet behind the ball. Because of the increased distance, your arm extension and finger alignment must be incredibly precise to account for the larger break required. Hold up the number of fingers that match your read, centering them on the hole to find your target line, then focus entirely on delivering the ball with enough speed to reach the "drop zone" just past the cup. The objective is to simplify complex, long-distance breaks into a single, confident starting line, ensuring your lag putting consistently leaves you with an easy second putt.

Select a hole on a green with a significant slope and place three markers exactly 5 feet away at different angles: one for a straight putt, one for a left-to-right break, and one for a right-to-left break. Your objective is to successfully hole three distinct types of putts from each location to master the relationship between line and speed. First, execute a "firm" putt that takes the straightest possible line and hits the back of the cup. Second, hit a "medium" putt with standard professional speed that captures the apex of the break and drops in at a moderate pace. Third, hit a "soft" putt that takes the maximum possible break and "dies" into the high side of the hole with almost no remaining momentum. If you miss any of the three speeds or fail to hole the putt from any of the three markers, you must reset the entire sequence. The goal is to complete all nine putts in a row, proving you can adapt your aim and delivery to any green condition.

Select a hole on a green with a consistent slope and place three markers exactly 10 feet away to test your speed control on medium-length putts. Your objective is to successfully hole three distinct types of putts from each marker to master how speed changes the line of the break. First, execute a "firm" putt that stays on a more direct line and hits the back of the cup with authority. Second, hit a "medium" putt with standard professional speed that allows the ball to take the natural break and drop in at a moderate pace. Third, hit a "soft" putt that takes the maximum possible break and "dies" into the high side of the hole with almost no remaining momentum. If you miss any of the three speeds or fail to hole the putt from any of the markers, you must reset the entire sequence to zero. The goal is to complete the full rotation at the 10-foot distance, proving you can intentionally adjust your entry speed and aim point on mid-range putts.

Select a hole on a green with a consistent slope and place three markers exactly 20 feet away to test your speed control on long-range putts. Your objective is to successfully hole three distinct types of putts from this distance to master the relationship between velocity and break. First, execute a "firm" putt that holds a tighter line and hits the back of the cup with enough pace to finish 18 inches past if it misses. Second, hit a "medium" putt with standard professional speed that allows the ball to take the full break and drop in at a dying pace. Third, hit a "soft" putt that takes the maximum possible "pro-side" break and barely trickles over the front edge of the hole. If you miss the hole or fail to deliver the ball at the specific intended speed, you must reset the entire sequence to zero. The goal is to prove you can intentionally manipulate the entry speed and starting line on 20-foot putts to navigate complex slopes.

Place a marker at the starting position exactly 5 feet from the hole. Your objective is to hit a series of putts using only your right hand while your left arm firmly holds your right bicep against your ribcage to prevent any independent arm movement. This setup is designed to stabilize the lead shoulder and ensure the putting stroke is driven entirely by the rotation of your torso and core. Focus on maintaining a consistent connection between your arm and body throughout the entire motion to produce a more repeatable and stable stroke at this critical distance. If you lose the physical connection between your arm and ribs or the ball misses the target, you must reset the sequence and start over from zero. The goal is to successfully hole a designated number of 5-foot putts in a row while maintaining perfect structural form and body-controlled timing.

Place a marker at the starting position exactly 5 feet from the hole. Your objective is to hit a series of putts using only your left hand while your right arm firmly holds your left bicep against your ribcage to prevent any independent arm movement. This setup is designed to stabilize the lead shoulder and ensure the putting stroke is driven entirely by the rotation of your torso and core. Focus on maintaining a consistent connection between your arm and body throughout the entire motion to produce a more repeatable and stable stroke at this critical distance. If you lose the physical connection between your arm and ribs or the ball misses the target, you must reset the sequence and start over from zero. The goal is to successfully hole a designated number of 5-foot putts in a row while maintaining perfect structural form and body-controlled timing.

Place an alignment stick vertically so one end is pressed firmly against the center of your chest and the other end is held against the back of the putter head. Your objective is to hit a series of putts while maintaining constant pressure between your chest and the putter throughout the entire stroke. This rigid connection forces the chest and torso to act as the primary power source, ensuring the putter head moves in perfect synchronization with your body's rotation. If the alignment stick loses contact with your chest or the putter head at any point, the stroke has broken down and you must reset the sequence to zero. The goal is to hole a designated number of putts in a row while keeping the stick perfectly stable, proving your chest is directing the path and pace of the stroke.

Place four markers exactly 3 feet from the hole at the North, South, East, and West positions to ensure you face uphill, downhill, left-to-right, and right-to-left putts. Your objective is to successfully hole one putt from each of the four locations in a continuous circle to prove total mastery of short-range breaking putts. By rotating around the hole, you are forced to recalibrate your read and adjust your speed for every single attempt. If you miss any of the four putts, the sequence is broken and you must reset to the first position and start the rotation over from zero. The goal is to complete a set number of full circles without a single miss, building the ultimate confidence and pressure-tested execution for putts inside the scoring zone.

Place four markers exactly 5 feet from the hole at the North, South, East, and West positions to ensure you face a complete variety of uphill, downhill, and side-hill breaks. Your objective is to successfully hole one putt from each of the four locations in a continuous circle to prove total mastery of mid-range scoring putts. By rotating around the hole at this specific distance, you are forced to recalibrate your aim and adjust your delivery speed for every single attempt. If you miss any of the four putts, the sequence is broken and you must reset to the first position and start the rotation over from zero. The goal is to complete a set number of full circles without a single miss, building the necessary pressure and confidence required to hole critical par-saving putts on the course.

Place four markers exactly 7 feet from the hole at the North, South, East, and West positions to ensure you face a complete variety of uphill, downhill, and side-hill breaks at a challenging distance. Your objective is to successfully hole one putt from each of the four locations in a continuous circle to prove total mastery of mid-range scoring putts where the break becomes more significant. By rotating around the hole at this specific 7-foot range, you are forced to recalibrate your aim and adjust your delivery speed for every single attempt. If you miss any of the four putts, the sequence is broken and you must reset to the first position and start the rotation over from zero. The goal is to complete a set number of full circles without a single miss, building the necessary pressure and confidence required to hole critical par-saving putts on the course.

Set up four markers at the North, South, East, and West positions around a single hole, ensuring you have a distinct uphill, downhill, left-to-right, and right-to-left putt. Your objective is to successfully hole three consecutive putts from each distance—3 feet, 5 feet, and then 7 feet—at every station for a total of 12 made putts in a row. This is a high-pressure drill designed to simulate the heat of a tournament round where every stroke carries significant weight.The pressure mounts as you progress; if you miss a single putt at any distance or any station, you must return to the very first 3-foot marker at the North station and reset your score to zero. You cannot move from the 3-foot to the 5-foot marker until you have holed the shorter one, and you cannot move to the next compass point until the 7-footer is made. The goal is to complete the full 12-putt rotation without a miss, forcing you to maintain absolute mechanical discipline and mental focus under the escalating threat of a total reset.
